1: An allusion: 'YHWH spoke to Moses, saying...' — the law of purification after childbirth is a divine speech act mediated through Moses. Christ as the greater mediator: 'The law was given through Moses; grace and truth came through Jesus Christ' (John 1:17). The laws that governed Christ's own birth and presentation (Leviticus 12:2-8) were received through Moses; their fulfillment came through the one who replaced Moses as the definitive word of God (Hebrews 1:1-2).
2: A direct type: the seven-day impurity of the mother after bearing a male child was fulfilled by Mary after Jesus's birth. 'When the time came for their purification according to the Law of Moses, they brought him up to Jerusalem to present him to the Lord' (Luke 2:22). Christ was born into the full weight of the law's requirements — 'born of woman, born under the law, to redeem those who were under the law' (Galatians 4:4-5). His mother's impurity was real; the law applied to the mother of the one who would fulfill the law.
3: A direct fulfillment: 'On the eighth day the flesh of his foreskin shall be circumcised.' Luke 2:21: 'At the end of eight days, when he was circumcised, he was called Jesus.' Christ received the sign of the Abrahamic covenant on the prescribed day, entering the covenant community through its founding rite. Paul: 'In him also you were circumcised with a circumcision made without hands, by putting off the body of the flesh, by the circumcision of Christ' (Colossians 2:11). The physical circumcision of Jesus is the ground of the spiritual circumcision he gives his people.
4: An allusion: thirty-three additional days of blood purification following the seven, totaling forty days before the mother can touch holy things or enter the sanctuary. Forty is the number of wilderness testing in Scripture: forty days of rain (Genesis 7:4), forty years in the desert, Moses's forty days on Sinai, Elijah's forty-day journey. Jesus fasted and was tested in the wilderness for forty days (Matthew 4:1-11) — the number of purification-through-testing runs from Leviticus to the desert of Jesus's temptation.
5: An allusion: the doubled purification period (fourteen plus sixty-six days = eighty total) for a female child reflects the greater blood-association of the female body in the Levitical system. The law applies to Mary's actual situation (a male child, forty days). The doubled period for a female child is never needed in the Gospel narrative — but theologically it raises the question of what the law required for the birth of the one who is 'neither male nor female' in Christ (Galatians 3:28). Christ's redemption transcends the male/female distinction the law employs.
6: A direct type: at the end of the purification period the mother brings a year-old lamb for a burnt offering and a pigeon or turtledove for a sin offering. Mary brought 'a pair of turtledoves or two young pigeons' (Luke 2:24) — the poor woman's substitute from Leviticus 12:8. The irony of the incarnation is complete here: the Lamb of God (John 1:29) is brought to the temple in the arms of a mother who could not afford a lamb. He who is the sacrifice arrives as the one for whose presentation the law requires a sacrifice.
7: A direct type: 'He [the priest] shall offer it before YHWH and make atonement for her, and she shall be clean.' The priest makes atonement and the woman is declared clean. Christ is the great High Priest who makes the ultimate atonement: 'how much more will the blood of Christ, who through the eternal Spirit offered himself without blemish to God, purify our conscience from dead works to serve the living God' (Hebrews 9:14). Every priestly act of atonement in Leviticus is the shadow; Christ's single self-offering is the substance (Hebrews 10:14: 'by a single offering he has perfected for all time those who are being sanctified').
8: A direct fulfillment: 'And if she cannot afford a lamb, then she shall take two turtledoves or two young pigeons.' Luke 2:24 quotes this verse directly: Mary and Joseph brought the poor-woman's substitute — 'a pair of turtledoves, or two young pigeons.' Paul applies the economic theology of the incarnation: 'For you know the grace of our Lord Jesus Christ, that though he was rich, yet for your sake he became poor, so that you by his poverty might become rich' (2 Corinthians 8:9). The King of kings was born into the household that brought the poorest offering — the ultimate condescension of the incarnation expressed in two small birds.
